title: Number of parties in multilateral environmental agreements
subtitle: Total number of global parties signed on to multilateral agreements designed to address trans-boundary environmental issues.
caption:
Number of parties in multilateral environmental agreements
Total number of global parties signed on to multilateral agreements designed to address trans-boundary environmental issues.

This line chart tracks the cumulative number of global parties signing onto thirteen specific multilateral environmental agreements between 1971 and 2015. The vertical axis measures the count of parties from 0 to 200, while the horizontal axis spans the years. The lines show a general upward trajectory for all listed treaties, with most reaching between 150 and 200 parties by the end of the period. The Vienna Convention on the Ozone Layer, represented by the dark blue line, begins in 1971 and rises to become the agreement with the highest number of parties, reaching just under 200 by 2015. The UN Framework Convention on Climate Change and the Convention on Biological Diversity, both starting in the early 1990s, follow closely behind, also approaching the 200-party mark. The UN Convention to Combat Desertification and the Kyoto Protocol show similar rapid growth after their inception in the mid-1990s, leveling off near 190 and 155 parties respectively. In contrast, the Convention on the Conservation of Migratory Species of Wild Animals, shown in dark green, starts in 1983 but grows more slowly, reaching approximately 125 parties by 2015. The Stockholm Convention on Persistent Organic Pollutants and the Cartagena Protocol on Biosafety begin around 2000 and rise steeply to roughly 170 and 160 parties. The Ramsar Convention on Wetlands and the Cites Convention show steady growth from the 1970s and 1980s, ending near 170 and 180 parties. The data shows that while start dates vary significantly, the majority of these environmental agreements attract a high volume of signatories within two to three decades of their establishment.
